title: Abolitionist Lawyer to File Amicus Brief in SCOTUS Abortion Case
author: Madison York
orientation: Announcing that attorney Bradley Pierce of the Foundation to Abolish Abortion will file an abolitionist amicus brief in Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization, the article lays out three arguments the brief will press: Fourteenth Amendment personhood and equal protection for the preborn, state Tenth Amendment authority to enforce homicide law, and Roe's illegitimacy as grounds for state defiance of SCOTUS.

questions.question: Why does the Foundation to Abolish Abortion consider this Dobbs amicus brief historically significant for the Abolitionist Movement?
questions.answer: Because despite dozens of post-Roe abortion cases reaching the Supreme Court, this filing represents the first time the Court will receive arguments framed explicitly from the abolitionist perspective rather than from incrementalist pro-life regulators.
questions.quote: There have been 39 abortion-related cases since *Roe v. Wade*, but for the first time, the Supreme Court will be hearing directly from the Abolitionist Movement.

questions.question: On what constitutional basis does Pierce argue that preborn children must receive equal protection?
questions.answer: He grounds the argument in the Fourteenth Amendment, citing the historical record that at the time of ratification states recognized human life as beginning at conception and made no distinction between human beings and persons.
questions.quote: When the amendment was ratified in 1868, [the states acknowledged](https://www.thepublicdiscourse.com/2017/08/19758/) both that human life begins at conception, and that there was no difference between human beings and persons.

questions.question: Why does the article reject Mississippi's HB 1510 as inadequate even though it claims to restrict abortion?
questions.answer: Because the bill submits to Roe's framework, leaves all preborn children younger than 15 weeks unprotected, depends on the abortionist self-reporting the killing, and imposes only trivial professional or financial penalties.
questions.quote: HB 1510 itself not only submits to *Roe*, but it does nothing to protect persons younger than 15 weeks gestation from being murdered, and thereby denies those people the equal protection of the law.

questions.question: What role does the Tenth Amendment play in the brief's argument about state authority?
questions.answer: It establishes that states retain police power to enforce homicide law against the killing of preborn children and need not await Supreme Court permission before protecting their citizens' right to life.
questions.quote: Second, according to the [Tenth Amendment](https://www.law.cornell.edu/constitution/tenth_amendment), states have the constitutional authority to use police power to control their own homicide enforcement.

questions.question: How does the article justify state defiance of a Supreme Court ruling?
questions.answer: It argues that Roe is an illegitimate, unconstitutional ruling that violates God's law, and that when SCOTUS exceeds its constitutional authority states have both the right and duty to nullify and interpose rather than defer.
questions.quote: Furthermore, the ruling is against God’s law, and states have the power to [defy its ruling](https://freethestates.org/nullification-and-interposition/index.html).

questions.question: What recent ecclesial action does the article cite as backing for state nullification of unjust court decisions?
questions.answer: It points to a Southern Baptist Convention resolution declaring that civil authorities have a duty to establish equal protection for the preborn by intervening in, ignoring, or nullifying unjust decisions like those of the Supreme Court.
questions.quote: “governing authorities at every level have a duty before God to uphold justice asserting their God-ordained and constitutional authority to establish equal protection under the law for all, born and preborn, by intervening, ignoring, or nullifying iniquitous decisions when other authorities, such as the Supreme Court, condone such injustices as the legal taking of innocent life.”

questions.question: What evidence does the article offer that the incrementalist pro-life litigation strategy has failed?
questions.answer: It notes that HB 1510 is the 40th pro-life law to reach SCOTUS in the 48 years since Roe, and not a single sentence of Roe has been rolled back, even with Republican-appointed majorities on the Court for nearly that entire span.
questions.quote: HB1510 is the 40th pro-life law to come before the Supreme Court in the 48 years since *Roe*, and not one sentence of the Roe decision has been rolled back

questions.question: What ultimatum does Pierce's brief deliver to the Supreme Court?
questions.answer: It does not merely request that Roe be overturned; it tells the Court that overturning Roe in full is constitutionally mandatory, and warns that abolitionists will pursue impeachment and state defiance if SCOTUS refuses.
questions.quote: He is telling them they must overturn it in full. Otherwise, we will utilize the constitutional checks and balances available to us: impeachment of corrupt officials rather than indifference towards them, and defiance of a tyrannical court rather than deference.

related.title: SCOTUS Arguments in Dobbs: Three Abolitionist Takeaways and One Prediction
related.reason: Direct follow-up reporting on the oral arguments in the same Dobbs case this brief targets.

related.title: 4 Abolitionist Rapid Reactions to the (Wrongly Decided) Dobbs Decision
related.reason: Closes the Dobbs arc by analyzing the actual ruling against the equal-protection arguments Pierce pressed.

related.title: Abolitionists Pass Resolution, Make Huge Strides at SBC Convention
related.reason: The SBC nullification resolution this article quotes at length.

related.title: Nullification
related.reason: Topical reference page on the nullification doctrine the brief invokes against SCOTUS.

related.title: Are Nullification’s Advocates the “Villains” of American History?
related.reason: Defends the historical legitimacy of state nullification, the constitutional remedy the brief relies on.

related.title: Supreme Court Idolatry at Texas Alliance for Life
related.reason: Companion critique of pro-life deference to SCOTUS, mirroring this article's failed-strategy argument.

related.title: Supreme Court Once Again Rejects Pro-Life Regulation
related.reason: Concrete prior example showing the regulatory pro-life strategy losing at SCOTUS, supporting Pierce's case for an abolitionist alternative.
